Items.Key: arbitrary command execution via admin shell|command injection|cwe-78|critical|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|605|608|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|if (command.substr(0, 4) == "exec") { std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|an authenticated administrator user (or anyone who successfully exploits the lack of proper authentication/override logic) can execute arbitrary os commands with the same privileges as the application.|providing an input starting with 'exec ' in the admin mode console.|avoid using system() with user-supplied strings. implement a whitelist of allowed commands or use a safer api like execve()->arbitrary command execution via admin shell|command injection|cwe-78|critical|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|605|608|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|if (command.substr(0, 4) == "exec") { std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|authenticated administrative users (or exploits bypasses) can execute arbitrary os commands with the application's privileges.|providing an input starting with 'exec ' via the administrative terminal.|replace system() with a hardcoded command manifest or use execve() with sanitized arguments.

illegal memory access / lifetime violation|use-after-return|cwe-416|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|218|222|string_utils::format_string|char buffer[buf_size]; snprintf(buffer, buf_size, fmt, args...); return buffer;|the function returns a pointer to a stack-allocated array that is destroyed as soon as the function returns. this results in undefined behavior and likely segmentation faults or arbitrary memory manipulation upon read/write.|any call to string_utils::format_string. |return a std::string instead of char* to ensure proper memory management.->illegal memory access / use-after-return|use-after-return|cwe-416|high|1|enhanced_calc.cpp|218|222|string_utils::format_string|char buffer[buf_size]; snprintf(buffer, buf_size, fmt, args...); return buffer;|the function returns a pointer/ref to an identifier representing a narrow stack array already out of scope. this can lead to uaf symptoms or undefined memory values after the function frame is popped.|calling `string_utils::format_string`|change function return type to `std::string`, which will force a copy construction into heap memory, ensuring the result outlives the function frame.
